dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 27-year-old male experiencing breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex. What got you calling 911 this morning?
I'm having a hard time breathing and think it's an asthma attack.
From dispatch, you're listed as having mild asthma. When was the last time you used your inhaler?
When did the symptoms start, and what feeling are you having?
Mr. Doe, what is today's date and where are we right now?
Can you tell me your phone number or address?
Can you squeeze both my hands to confirm your motor response?
I'm going to measure your blood pressure now.
I'll also check your oxygen level with the pulse oximeter.
I can hear wheezing in both my stethoscope and my ears. May I listen more carefully?
Your pulse is 81-strong, regular, and normal. Your skin looks warm, dry, and intact.
Your airway is clear. Is it hard for you to speak or feel hoarse?
Your breathing is labored with wheezing in both inspiration and expiration.
Just to be safe, I'll check your blood sugar.
I need to place some ECG leads on your chest for cardiac monitoring.
Have you ever had asthma attacks like this before? When was the last time you used your inhaler?
The last attack happened five days ago. I used the inhaler this morning but it ran out.
Are you experiencing anything else besides trouble breathing?
I have back pain when I take deep breaths. It's sharp and aching, around a 7/10.
When did the back pain begin? Is it connected to your breathing?
Does it get worse when you lay down or move around at all?
It starts sharp and feels more like an aching pain now. It doesn't spread.
Can you point exactly where it hurts you? It's more in your upper back or shoulders?
On a 10-point scale, how bad would you say it is now?
Your breathing rate is 22 and it still sounds labored.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-AOx4.
Still seeing wheezing during both breathing in and out.
Impressive-your pulses and circulation are still stable with nothing new developing.
ECG is still showing normal sinus rhythm and no changes yet.
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive-nothing out of place or injury.
How is the pain in your back now, Mr. Doe?
It's holding steady at a 7 and the wheezing feels a little better.
Any new pain you haven't mentioned yet?
No new symptoms yet. It's the same sharp, aching feeling as before.
I've given you 0.25mg of Terbutaline under your skin for your breathing.
You were using albuterol regularly for your asthma, but you ran out about a day ago.
I've also started a 4-lead ECG for continuous heart monitoring.
I'm putting in an IV in one of the veins in your arm or leg-it'll help manage things if needed.
This fits the asthma protocol-no need to switch protocols at this point.
You're stable to transport. We're taking you to VCU ER as you requested.
Let's get you safely laid out on the stretcher.
I'll secure all the seatbelts to keep you safe while we move.
Upon arrival, I'll give the report to the ER. Still nothing new or worse reported.
The patient was transported directly to ED-TA Bed 15.
This is a 27-year-old male with mild asthma and acute respiratory distress.
Vital signs included: pulse 81, Bp 125/78, SpO2 96%, and glucose 81.
EKG found normal sinus rhythm using 4-lead monitoring.
Terbutaline 0.25mg subcutaneous was administered as treatment.
An IV was placed in a 20G catheter in a peripheral vein-same arm.
The ride-along form was signed by the patient and confirmed complete.
We're turning it over with a field impression of asthma exacerbation and upper back discomfort.
Pupils were noted as equal, round, and reactive-PEARL.
Everything's up to date on our end. Is there anything further from you?
All looks good. Thanks for the details.
